nodes.text: Sunday
Diego wakes at four-oh-eight PM.

The blackout curtains are doing what they have been doing since he was sixteen, which is keeping his bedroom in the kind of dark that has no relationship to the time of day outside it. He lies on his back for a beat. He registers the quality of the dark and registers that the quality of the dark is the four-PM quality, which is the recovery-Sunday quality, which is the day after inventory.
He gets up.
He goes downstairs.

[shot:closeup abuela]His grandmother is at the kitchen table with the Sentinel. She has, in front of her, the small bowl of fruit she eats in the late afternoon when she has decided that her body needs the fruit before dinner more than it needs anything else.
She looks up when he comes in.
There is a plate in the microwave.
Thank you, abuela.

He microwaves the plate. He sits across from her. The plate is enchiladas — the same enchiladas she has been making since he was four — and the small side of black beans she does with the cumin and the lime.
He eats.
Tomorrow is school.
Yes.
Senior year.
Senior year.
You are ready.
Maybe. I don't know. I am — I will go in the morning, and I will see.
That is the only way to be ready.
[shot:closeup diego]He smiles, small.
He eats the rest of the plate.
After dinner he goes back upstairs. He sits at the desk in his bedroom that has been his desk since he was nine. He pulls out a clean sheet of plain white printer paper. He pulls out the same pen.
[shot:insert letter]He writes the second letter.
Papá.
I am writing again like I said I would. Tomorrow is the first day of school. I am a senior. The summer was — the summer was a thing. I am okay.

I have been thinking about you. I have been wondering how the work is. I have been wondering whether the avocados came in well this season — I remember you used to write to me about the avocados when I was small, and I have been thinking about those letters this summer.
Tell me about the town. Tell me about anything.
I am going to write again in two weeks. By then I will have something to tell you about school. By then maybe you will have written something back. By then we will see.
Yo te quiero, Papá.
Tu hijo, Diego.

He folds the letter. He puts it in an envelope. He addresses the envelope. He puts the envelope on the corner of the desk where he will find it Wednesday morning when he leaves for the post office before school.
He closes the desk drawer.
He goes back downstairs.

[shot:establish~]His grandmother is on the front porch. The cicadas are doing their early-evening warm-up. The sky is the long lavender of a Texas Sunday in mid-August. He sits in the second porch chair beside her.
They do not talk.
They sit.
In the next cul-de-sac over, the Caldwell front yard at three forty-five PM has been arranged for the small specific event Anita has been organizing for two weeks.

[shot:insert cake]The folding table from the Kowalski garage is set up under the pecan tree. On the table is the cake — a sheet cake from the bakery on Magnolia, with white frosting and the words GRACIE — STICKER QUEEN in pink piping. Beside the cake, on a small folded cloth, sits Gracie's completed activity book — the Sunday-school-issued sticker-and-puzzle book. It is the third book Gracie has finished this summer. It is the one for which there is, by Anita's small declaration two weeks ago, going to be a small ceremony on the Sunday before school starts.

[shot:establish]Linda is in the wicker chair from the Caldwell porch, which Maya has wheeled out to the yard. Anita is at the table, in her good Sunday blouse — the blue one — pouring lemonade into paper cups. Bill is by the cake, in his good Sunday shirt, doing the small specific Bill-thing of pretending he is annoyed about the ceremony while being, by anyone's read who has known him for more than ten minutes, deeply pleased.
Gracie is in a sundress.

The Hendersons arrive at three fifty-two. Eileen has the cinnamon coffee cake in the small white box from the bakery. Jim is beside her, in the careful clothes he has been wearing this summer for any public appearance. Jesse is not there — he is at home, by his own choice, sleeping off the gig. Eileen explains this to Anita at the lemonade table, and Anita says that Jesse is forgiven on the basis of the gig itself, which Anita had heard about from Bianca who had heard about it from Eileen at the four-thirty table on Friday and which Anita had then, all weekend, been telling her own night-shift coworkers about.

Ben arrives at three fifty-six. He is in the same clothes he wore to the Caldwell porch on Friday night, plus a clean t-shirt and, by his own small Sunday concession to the occasion, a button-down over the t-shirt that he has not buttoned.
Maya is on the lawn near her grandmother's chair.
The thing begins at four.
Bill, at the cake, taps the side of a paper cup with a plastic fork — the small specific gesture of a man making an announcement at a small backyard ceremony.
Okay. So. Gracie. Come up here.
[shot:closeup gracie]Gracie comes up. She stands beside the table, beside her sticker book, beside her father.

Gracie, this summer, decided in early June that she was going to do the sticker book that the Sunday school had given her. The Sunday school had told the kids it was an optional summer activity. Gracie, who has, since she was four, taken instructions from the Sunday school more seriously than the Sunday school takes them itself, decided that the optional was not optional. She started the book on June ninth. She finished it last Tuesday.
It was not even hard.
She also did the second book, which the Sunday school did not give her but which she found at the small bookstore in New Auburn in early July and which she insisted I buy.
He bought it.
I bought it. She finished the second book on July thirty-first.
Also not hard.

And the third book she did was the activity book that her aunt sent her for her birthday in March and that we had thought she had already finished and that turned out to be unfinished. She did that one in two weeks.
That one was a little hard.
Three sticker books, one summer. We are, today, eating cake. Then we are taking a picture. Then everybody can go home.
The small group claps.
Eileen, beside Jim, leans into him.

Jim, beside Eileen, smiles — the slow careful Jim-smile of a man who is, this Sunday afternoon, in the front yard of a neighbor's house at a child's ceremony, and is registering in his interior that he is in the front yard of a neighbor's house at a child's ceremony.
Anita cuts the cake.

[shot:insert photo]The picture, at four eleven, is taken by Maya with Anita's phone. The picture is Gracie in the center, holding the third sticker book up, with Bill at her left and Anita at her right, the cake in front of them on the table. The picture is, by Maya's framing, slightly off-center to the left, which Maya does not notice and which Anita, when she looks at the picture later, decides she likes more than she would have liked the centered version.
The picture takes three takes. The third take is the one Anita keeps.
The cake gets eaten.

Eileen's cinnamon coffee cake, which Anita had not known about until Eileen arrived with it, gets cut in half and saved for Wednesday — the day Anita has, on her work calendar, marked as the first day she will have to pack a lunch for her shift, because the schedule has rotated.
Gracie eats two pieces of cake.
The thing ends at four forty-three.

[shot:establish]Ben drives Linda back to the porch with the wicker chair in the back of the Civic. Maya rides in the passenger seat. At the Caldwell porch Linda gets out. Maya helps her up the steps and into the wicker. Ben unloads the chair to the porch, sets it back where it had been on Friday evening.
Sit, Benjamin.
I should — I should head home. I have to be in bed by ten.
You have time. Sit.
He sits.
Maya brings out three iced teas.
The three of them sit on the porch in the quiet of Sunday at five-oh-eight PM.
School tomorrow.
Yeah.
Yeah.
You two are going to be in the same school for one more year. After that — Benjamin will be somewhere. Maya will be in this town. The two of you will figure it out.
Yeah.
Yeah.

[shot:closeup linda]I have, in seventy-eight years, watched many friendships of seventeen-year-olds. The ones that hold are the ones where the two people in question understand, by August before senior year, that the next several years are going to ask a different shape of the friendship than the previous ones did. The understanding does not, by itself, hold the friendship. But the not-understanding usually breaks it.
Linda. Thank you.
I am not telling you anything you didn't know. I am just saying it out loud.
Yeah.
The three of them sit for another fifteen minutes.
Ben gets up at five twenty-three. He hugs Linda. He hugs Maya. He drives home.
[shot:closeup maya]Maya, on the porch alone with her grandmother after Ben has gone, drinks her iced tea.
I'm going to ask Rick tomorrow.
About working through senior year?
Yeah.
He'll say yes.
I think so too. I'm going to ask anyway. I don't want to assume.
Good. Asking is the right move.
Two days a week. Saturdays plus one weeknight. Probably Wednesdays.
Wednesday is good. Wednesday is the middle. Wednesday is the day the week settles.
Yeah.
They sit.
[shot:establish]In another part of town, at the same hour, Bianca is at her own kitchen table.

Sammy is across from her. Sammy has, on the table, a small half-finished bowl of cereal that Bianca had made Sammy promise to actually eat before they had the conversation Sammy had asked them to have on Sunday afternoon before school started.
Sammy is, this afternoon, in the soft cotton shorts and the old t-shirt she wears at home on Sundays.
Okay. You wanted to talk.
Yeah.
Sammy eats a spoonful of cereal.
[shot:closeup sammy]Mom. I wanted to tell you that I am going to apply early to UT-Austin.
Okay.
I had been thinking about it all summer. I decided last week. I wanted to tell you on Sunday before school started so you would know going into the year.
Okay.
Early decision is binding. If I get in, I'm going. There is no — there is no opt-out clause.
I know how early decision works, Sammy.
Yeah. I know you know. I just — I wanted to say it out loud.
[shot:closeup bianca]Bianca, at the table, is for a beat very still.
You should apply early.
Yeah?

Yeah. UT-Austin is the right school for you. The early-decision program is the right way to go for the kind of student you are. The binding part is the part that will, by November, force you to be honest with yourself about whether you actually want to go there. You will, by then, know. If you submit the early-decision application, you have decided. If you do not, you have also decided. Either way, you will, by November, have decided.
Okay.
Sammy.
Yeah.

I want you to go to UT-Austin. I have wanted you to go to UT-Austin since you were ten years old and we drove down for that football game and you spent the entire weekend asking your father about the architecture of the engineering quad. I am going to be — I am going to be very, very glad if you get in. I am also going to be very, very sad to have you gone. The two feelings are going to live in me at the same time. I want you to know that the two feelings are going to live in me at the same time. I do not want you to make the decision based on either one of them. The decision is yours. The feelings are mine.
Okay, Mom.
Apply early.
Yeah.
The two of them sit at the table.
Sammy eats another spoonful of cereal.
Mom.
Yeah.
When I'm in Austin. I'm going to come home a lot. I'm — I'm not going to be the kind of college student who doesn't come home.
Sammy. You don't have to promise that.
I know. I'm telling you anyway.
Okay.
I want to be the kind of kid who comes home.
[shot:closeup bianca~]Bianca, at the table, looks at her daughter for a long beat.
Okay, Sammy. Come home.
After Sammy goes upstairs to do the small things she does on Sunday evenings — laundry, folding, bag-packing for the first day — Bianca stays at the table.
[shot:insert stationery]She gets a clean piece of stationery from the small drawer where she keeps the stationery she has not used since 2018. She gets a pen.
She writes the letter back to Mike.
Mike,

I got your letter from the second week of August. I have been sitting with it. I am writing back today because tomorrow is the first day of school and I wanted you to have something from me from this side of the summer.

Sammy is going to apply early to UT-Austin. She told me this afternoon. She told me on the Sunday before school started because she wanted me to know going in. I think she is going to get in. I think she should. I told her so. I am — I am going to be a wreck about it in November. She knows. She is going anyway and she is right to.

I have been doing a thing on Friday mornings with two of the women in the cul-de-sac. Eileen Henderson and Anita Kowalski. We sit at the table at four-thirty AM. We do not perform okay for each other. It is the closest thing I have to the consolation of having other adults in the room.
I am going to plant a garden in March. I have not had a garden since 2009. I have decided.
Carlos sent me the radio you used to listen to on the porch in the early years. It will be at the house Wednesday. I do not know what I am going to do with it. I will figure it out.

I think about you. I think about you on the days the schedule says I am allowed to and I think about you on the days the schedule does not. The schedule is a thing I made up to get through the summer. It worked. I am, by the end of the summer, in a different shape than I was in May. Some of the shape is the schedule. Some of it is the four-thirty-table. Some of it is Sammy. Some of it is — I do not entirely know what.

Tomorrow Sammy starts senior year. I will be at the kitchen window watching her drive away in the car you bought her in 2023. I will, after she is gone, sit with my coffee for a long time. The sitting is what I do.
October is the next phone call. I will be ready.
Be well.
Bianca.

She folds the letter. She puts it in an envelope. She addresses the envelope to the facility in Alabama, with the small specific routing line Carlos had written down for her in May and that she has been using since.
She puts the envelope on the corner of the kitchen counter where she will see it tomorrow morning when she makes her coffee.
She rinses Sammy's bowl.
She puts the bowl in the dishwasher.
She turns out the kitchen light.
